title: Adverse Selection and Climate Risk: A Response toOuazad and Kahn (2022)
abstract: We identify two issues in the work ofOuazad and Kahn (2022). Correcting either reverses the original result. The two changes are to use the correct FHFA conforming loan limits for each county and year and to compare the individual loan amount to that limit correctly. There is no evidence that lenders transfer climate risk by altering loan origination and securitization behavior. None of our results calls into question the value and importance of the O&K model as a test for adverse selection. The question addressed and the setup of the test are important and should be replicated over time.

author.Andrey Pavlov*: Beedie School of Business, Simon Fraser University,Canada
author.Susan Wachter: The Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania,USA

article.1 Replication of Original Result:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Matvos Gregor Matvos Gregor Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Ouazad and Kahn (2022)state that mortgage lenders change their lending behaviour following a major hurricane. Specifically, O&K state that lenders originate more loans that are eligible for securitization and also securitize a larger portion of the loans in affected areas. If true, this finding would imply that institutions that securitize mortgages are subject to adverse selection.

We repeat O&K’s analysis using their original data and code and our own entirely independent data retrieval and methodology. In doing so, we identify two data issues in the original work. Correcting these limitations reverses the findings reported in O&K’s main result (reported in theirtable 2). After applying these data corrections to O&K’s original data and code, 2 of the 36 post-event coefficients remain positive and significant.1Our independent implementation removes all significance.

This is the only change in the code. After this change, 11 (rather than 25) of the 36 post-event coefficients remain positive and significant. Refer to Table 1 for a description of the reported parameters.

The Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA) specifies precise loan amount limits for a loan to be eligible for acquisition by the two Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs), Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. This limit is known as the “conforming loan limit” and is a statutory requirement.2For instance, the conforming limit in Collier County, Florida, in 2012 was $448,500, meaning that only loans originated up to and including this amount were eligible for acquisition. These loan limits vary by county and through time.

These precise conforming loan limits generate a well-documented discontinuity of loan originations around the limit. O&K use this discontinuity to find that the proportion of loans originated just below the limit increases relative to the proportion of loans originated just above the limit in affected areas following a major hurricane. If correct, such a finding would suggest that loan originators alter their behavior to make more loans eligible for securitization in affected areas.

We document two issues in O&K’s data processing. First, their final data used for model estimation contains only two conforming limits for each year – “standard” and “high-cost”. For instance, in 2012, all counties in their data have limits of either $417,000 or $625,500. While the standard limit at the time was indeed $417,000, each “high-cost” county had its own specific limit. For instance, the FHFA limit for Collier County, Florida, in 2012 was $448,500, while the limit in O&K’s data for that county was incorrectly set at $625,500. Similarly, O&K’s data has two limits for all years, including the 1995 - 2006 period, when there were no “high-cost” counties in the states of interest.

The second issue in O&K’s data is in the comparison of each loan amount to the conforming limit. The HMDA loan application data contain loan amounts rounded to the nearest $1,000, while the FHFA loan limits are reported to the dollar. For instance, the standard conforming loan limit in 2005 was $359,650. Loan applications near or at the limit that year are reported in the HMDA data as having a loan amount of “360” to denote a rounded amount of $360,000. Thus, a direct comparison of the loan amount ($360,000) to the conforming limit ($359,650) results in misclassification of the loan as being above-limit. In the following year, 2006, the conforming limit changed to $417,000, which would not generate any misclassification. In this example, the originations below the limit would appear to have substantially increased from 2005 to 2006 even though there was no such increase. While this is only one specific example, it is noteworthy because five hurricanes occurred in 2005, and the apparent increase in below-limit originations was incorrectly attributed to an effect from these hurricanes rather than to the change in loan classification from 2005 to 2006.

Sections 1 - 5 will detail the above issues and quantify their significance and prevalence in the data. After correcting these issues, using O&K’s code, two of the 36 post-event coefficients remain positive and significant.3Our independent implementation removes all positive significance. Therefore, there is no evidence of unpriced climate risk, as O&K originally state.

Ouazad and Kahn (2023), henceforth O&K23, contains a reply to an earlier version of this paper.Internet Appendix Dprovides a detailed analysis of O&K23 and explains why we believe our criticisms still hold as does the reversal of their results.

None of our results calls into question the value and importance of the O&K model as a test for adverse selection. The question O&K address and the setup of the test are important and should be replicated over time. As climate-related disasters increase in magnitude and frequency, lenders may take steps to reduce their exposure to risk areas. One way of doing so would be to increase the securitization of loans originated in risk areas. Therefore, we believe that continuous monitoring of lending and securitization in risk areas using the O&K model is prudent.

We proceed as follows:

Section 1: Replicates O&K’stable 2using their data and code with no changes. In O&K’s original result, 25 of the 36 post-event coefficients are significant.4

Section 2: Addresses the rounding issue in O&K’s original code. Following this change alone, 11 (rather than 25) of the 36 post-event coefficients remain positive and significant.5Nine coefficients switch sign.

Section 3: Replicates O&K’stable 2(also provided in Section 1) using source HMDA data but retaining the rounding and the conforming limit issues in the code. This replicates the original result exactly or very closely depending on the model. (We must do this because we need the source data to correct the conforming limit).

Section 4: Repeats Section 3 but corrects the conforming limit issue. This section retains the rounding issue. After correcting the limit issue alone, 7 of the 36 post-event coefficients remain positive and significant.6Ten coefficients switch sign.

Section 5: Repeats Section 3 but corrects both the rounding and the conforming limit issues. After correcting both issues, 2 of the 36 post-event coefficients remain positive and significant. Twelve coefficients switch sign.

Section 6: Tests the O&K hypothesis using our own entirely independent data retrieval and analysis that alters (and in our opinion, improves) further details of the data construction and regression specification and provides for extensive robustness analysis. No coefficients are positive and significant in this implementation.
Table 1replicates exactly O&K’s main result (table 2in their paper) using the data and code package the authors provided to us. The estimated model is provided in O&K equation (1) and also reproduced inInternet Appendix B.
article.2 Addressing the Rounding Issue:
We first address the rounding issue described above. In the code O&K provided to us, we believe the incorrect comparison occurs on line 80 of the file named:

2_analysis_hmda/04_make_hmda_event_study_data_set.R.

This line is as follows:

diff_log_loan_amount = log_loan_amount - log(effective_loanlimit/1000),

where,log_loan_amountis the HMDA rounded loan amount. Thus, this line compares the loan amount from the HMDA data (which is rounded to the nearest $1,000) to the conforming loan limit (which is rounded to the dollar). Substituting a loan amount and conforming limit of $359,650 in the line of code above generates the following expression:

diff_log_loan_amount = log(360) - log(359650/1000).

This calculation generates a positive number, which in turn incorrectly classifies the loan as being above the conforming limit.

The correct comparison rounds the conforming loan limit in the second term to the nearest $1,000, so that the variable”diff_log_loan_amount”becomes zero and the loan is correctly classified as being at or below the conforming limit. This method applies to the conforming loan limit the same rounding HMDA applies to loan amounts.

Figure 1quantifies the scope of the misclassification. The figure reports the number of correctly classified and misclassified originations in the +/– 5% of conforming limit sample.

Fig. 1 Prevalence of misclassified loans The figure depicts the number of correctly classified and misclassified originations from the +/–5% sample. Open in new tab Download slide

The extent of this misclassification is also evident infigure 2aprovided by O&K. Below, we reproduce this figure from the original paper (left panel) and also provide an equivalent figure that correctly classifies all applications (right panel). The original figure (left) has an unusually high observation count just above the limit. Since almost no loans are ever originated just above the conforming limit, the count just above the limit should be very low. Our replication (right) shows that indeed this count is very low.

Fig. 2 Replication of O&K figure 2a The figure reproduces O&K’s figure 2a (left) and our replication (right). The original figure shows a high count of applications just above the limit, which is not realistic. Our replication shows that the count of applications just above the limit is very low, as expected. Open in new tab Download slide

Note that applying to the conforming limit the same rounding HMDA applies to loan amounts misclassifies loan applications just above the limit as below limit. Using the above example, loan applications between $359,651 and $360,499, inclusive, also would be rounded to $360,000 in HMDA and would be misclassified as at or below the limit in our procedure. However, since loans bunch at or below the limit, correctly classifying loans at or below the limit is far more important than misclassifying the few, if any, loans just above the limit.7

Table 2reproducestable 1, except it corrects the rounding issue discussed above. After this change, 11 (rather than 25) of the 36 post-event coefficients remain positive and significant.8Nine coefficients switch sign and become negative.

Once we correct for the conforming loan limits as well, as we do below, all but two coefficients lose their significance. No coefficients remain positive and significant in our independent implementation.

As an alternative to directly correcting the rounding issue, we also make the original regression robust to misclassification near the conforming limits. Specifically, we reestimate the regression using O&K’s data and code but eliminate observations that fall within 1% of the conforming limit. For instance, the +/–5% sample becomes -5% to -1% and +1% to +5%. The results of this procedure are similar to those intable 2and are reported inInternet Appendix A.

Similarly, we also reestimate the regression using O&K’s data and code but eliminate all observations with HMDA-rounded loan amount equal to the rounded conforming limit. This also makes the original O&K estimation robust to the rounding issue. The results of this procedure are also similar to those intable 2and reported inInternet Appendix A.9
article.3 Replication from HMDA Data:
In this section, we address the issue of conforming loan limits. Those limits, determined by FHFA, prescribe the maximum loan amount for conforming loans (Federal Housing Financing Agency. n.d.). It is those conforming loan limits that form the basis of O&K’s identification strategy. The conforming loan limits are publicly available and vary by county and year. Many counties have the “standard” limit for the year. However, higher home price counties are assigned a “high-cost” limit, which varies by county and year.

Table 3provides an example of several counties that had a variety of conforming limits in 2012. In O&K’s analysis, the “high-cost” counties here have the same incorrect conforming limit of $625,500, which is simply 50% higher than the standard limit of $417,000. In reality, the loan limits vary substantially across counties. This discrepancy is important because using the incorrect limit then incorrectly classifies loans as above- and below-limit.

In O&K’s analysis, many of these counties were given the same limit of $625,500. Note that Columbia, Florida, was not a “high-cost” county.

Prior to 2007, there were no “high-cost” counties in the Atlantic states of interest, and all counties in these states had the same conforming limit. The limits only varied by year. However, in O&K’s data, we find two distinct conforming limits for each year.table 4lists the two limits O&K use and the single FHFA limit.

During the 1995-2006 period, no “high-cost” counties were in the states of interest.

The data package O&K provided to us starts with a processed data file that already has the incorrect limits hard-coded. Therefore, we are unable to address the limits issue directly in O&K’s code. Instead, we re-construct the analysis starting from the raw HMDA data. We follow O&K’s procedure as closely as possible. We retain their definition and identification of treatment areas.

Table 5reports our closest replication of O&K’stable 2(replicated astable 1), including the rounding and the conforming limit issue. The only difference is that we reconstruct the analysis from the raw HMDA data, rather than the data file provided to us.

Our replication of the +/– 20% sample results in similar coefficient sign and significance (columns 1, 4, and 7). However, the coefficient estimates and sample sizes are different. We believe this difference arises from an incorrect selection of the +/– 20% sample in the original paper. O&K’stable 2, replicated here intable 1above, reports a virtually identical number of observations for the +/– 10% and the +/– 20% subsamples. In reality, the bigger sample should have many more observations, which is the case in our replication reported intable 5.

This table retains the rounding issue. After correcting the limit issue, 7 (rather than 25) of the 36 post-event coefficients remain positive and significant. Ten coefficients reverse sign.

After correcting the limit issue alone, 7 of the 36 post-event coefficients remain positive and significant.10Ten post-event coefficients switch sign and become negative.

All models reported intable 6have substantially more observations than the results reported intable 5. This is not because of any changes in the data. Instead, loan applications below the conforming limit far exceed loan applications above the limit, as suggested byFigure 2. Therefore, selecting samples around the correct conforming limits results in a far greater number of observations than selecting samples around an arbitrary point above the correct limit.

As a robustness verification of the above result, we repeat O&K’s original estimation, using their data and code, but removing all counties which at some point in the sample were identified as high-cost. Doing so reduces the number of observations but makes the original regression robust to the incorrect conforming limits.Internet Appendix Aprovides the detailed results from this robustness check. Only one of the 36 post-event coefficients remain positive and significant once high-cost counties are excluded.

These are the only two changes relative to Table 5. After correcting the two issues, 2 (rather than 25) post-event coefficients remain positive and significant. Twelve coefficients switch sign and become negative.

All models reported intable 7have substantially more observations than the results reported intable 5. As already discussed in the previous section, this is not because of any changes in the data. Instead, loan applications below the conforming limit far exceed loan applications above the limit. Thus, using the correct limit increases the number of observations without any change in the data.

Correcting the rounding and the conforming limit issues eliminates the significance of all but 2 of the 36 post-event coefficients.11Twelve of the thirty-six coefficients switch sign. Two coefficients become negative and significant.

The two coefficients that remain positive and significant are at time T+3 in the Approved model. First, of 36 coefficients, we can expect one or two to be significant by chance. Second, it is difficult to imagine that lenders do not change their lending practices one and two years following a hurricane, but do so three years later, and only for one year. Third, while two coefficients remain positive and significant, two coefficients become negative and significant. This all suggests that the significant outcomes are due to chance rather than a true relationship.
article.6 Independent Implementation:
This section tests the O&K hypothesis using our own entirely independent data retrieval and analysis that alters (and in our opinion, improves) further details of the data construction and regression specification and provides for extensive robustness analysis.12We follow O&K’s general approach but our approach differs from theirs along several details as follows:

The original work uses ZIP codes. However, the HMDA, wind path, elevation, and distance to water data are all available at the census tract level. Since conversion from census tracts to ZIP codes is not unique or trivial, we perform our independent implementation at the census tract level.It appears observations in the original work are assigned a treatment fixed effect only if they fall in the T-4 to T+4 time window. Since this is a location fixed effect, unrelated to time, we assign treatment fixed effects to all observations that fall in a treated census tract to capture potential geographic systemic differences that are unrelated to the impact of hurricanes.It appears loan applications in affected ZIP codes that were outside of the T-4 to T+4 window were completely excluded by O&K. Instead, we include them as part of the control group.Observations treated by multiple hurricanes were repeated for each hurricane by O&K. This does not create multicollinearity because different indicator variables take the value of one, but the observations are identical in all other variables. We modify this by setting two or more treatment indicator variables to one in the same observation as needed, which we believe is the standard approach for this situation.The original estimation includes a separate indicator variable for “high-cost” counties, along with interaction variables between this variable and year. The paper does not specify or explain this, and it is not clear to us why a separate indicator is needed. We have eliminated this from our estimation.The files provided through HMDA have two versions: “final,” which is a frozen version of the original submission, and “ultimate,” which includes post-submission changes and amendments. The original estimation uses a mix of “final” and “ultimate” HMDA files. We use the “ultimate” files throughout.

We detail our modelling and data selection choices inInternet Appendix B, where we also highlight some additional technical departures from O&K’s work.

Table 8reports the results. This implementation includes a substantially higher number of observations and we believe identifies treated observations with greater precision. The overall results, however, do not change substantially relative totable 7. We find no positive and significant coefficients in the T+1 to T+4 estimates. On the contrary, most coefficients switch sign and sometimes even become negative and significant. Overall, these results offer no indication of adverse selection following a hurricane.

It also includes several additional refinements.

In addition to the above results, we perform an extensive robustness analysis, which we detail inInternet Appendix C. Altogether, we attempt over 100 model specifications and data subsamples. None of those alternatives generates results indicating the presence of adverse selection or unpriced climate risk in the data.
article.7 Conclusion:
We document that the claims presented by O&K are based on two incorrect treatments of the data. Correcting these reverses the original O&K results. We find this both in O&K’s original data and code and in our own independent implementation.

This finding means that we have no evidence that lenders change their origination or securitization behavior in affected areas following a major hurricane. Therefore, adverse selection in the securitization process is not detectable in the data.

None of our results calls into question the value and importance of the O&K model as a test for adverse selection. The question O&K address and the setup of the test are important and should be replicated over time because the hypothesized risk transfer and adverse selection could occur in the future. Increased frequency and magnitude of extreme climate events make the assessment of their risk easier going forward and increase the incentives to transfer it to other market participants. Therefore, there is a clear need to design and implement mechanisms and procedures that monitor for potential risk transfer.
